questions.questionText: A 55-year-old male smoker has progressive hoarseness for 3 months. Indirect laryngoscopy shows a growth on the right vocal cord. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx (most common laryngeal malignancy — glottic carcinoma presents with early hoarseness, best prognosis due to early detection and limited lymphatic drainage)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Progressive hoarseness in smoker → laryngeal carcinoma until proven otherwise. Why correct: SCC = >95% of laryngeal cancers. Glottic (vocal cord) = most common site (60-65%). Early hoarseness → early detection. Supraglottic: presents late (throat pain, referred otalgia, neck mass). Risk factors: smoking, alcohol, HPV. Why A wrong: Vocal cord nodules — bilateral, at anterior 1/3, in vocalists ('singer's nodules'). Why C wrong: Papilloma — benign, HPV 6/11, children (recurrent respiratory papillomatosis), adults (single). Why D wrong: Reinke's edema — polypoid degeneration from smoking, bilateral diffuse. Clinical relevance: Glottic carcinoma: best prognosis (early detection + poor lymphatic drainage = late metastasis). Stage T1 glottic: radiation therapy (90%+ cure rate). Exam trap: Glottic = early hoarseness, BEST prognosis. Supraglottic = LATE symptoms, WORST prognosis. Subglottic = RAREST. Memory aid: 'Glottic = early Grumble (hoarseness) = Good prognosis.' Reference: Dhingra – Larynx.
questions.conceptTag: Laryngeal carcinoma glottic SCC hoarseness smoker
